starter.label: Starter
starter.description: Hardy, forgiving plants. Hard to kill, tolerant of beginner mistakes, widely available.

pairs.reason: Both tolerate a wide humidity range and low light; pothos roots won't rot even if the moss keeps the base consistently damp.
Similar low-light, low-fuss needs; sheet moss controls surface moisture evaporation, which pothos doesn't mind either way.
Peperomia prefers slightly drier soil than moss wants, but in an open terrarium the airflow keeps both happy.
Pilea likes bright indirect light and moderate moisture, matching moss's preferences closely enough for open setups.
Fittonia wilts fast when dry, so the humidity moss helps retain actually benefits it.
Both are dense, moisture-loving groundcovers with matching light and water needs — a very safe starter pairing.
Baby's Tears stays low and won't compete with pothos for light; both tolerate similar watering schedules.
Similar light and drainage needs; neither is aggressive enough to crowd the other out.
Both want consistent moisture and low-to-medium light — one of the easiest pairings for closed terrariums.
Pothos can vine above while fittonia stays low; no competition for the same growing space.

intermediate.label: Intermediate
intermediate.description: Plants with more specific humidity, light, or drainage needs. Require some monitoring and occasional adjustment.

pairs.reason: Both need consistently high humidity and no direct sun, but maidenhair fern will brown quickly if the terrarium dries out even briefly — needs monitoring.
Compatible moisture needs, but button fern grows slower and can get shaded out if fittonia isn't trimmed back.
Polka dot plant wants brighter light than moss prefers; works only if light is filtered, not direct.
Maranta needs more airflow at the leaf surface than a moss-heavy closed setup allows — leaves can develop fungal spotting.
Sheet moss keeps soil wetter than peperomia's semi-succulent roots tolerate long-term, risking root rot in closed containers.
Similar shade and moisture needs, though baby's tears grows faster and needs regular trimming to avoid smothering the fern base.
Both thrive in high humidity; the fern's rhizomes benefit from moss holding surface moisture, but need visible rhizome space, not full moss coverage.
Nearly identical moisture and light needs, but selaginella spreads aggressively and can outcompete moss without regular trimming.
Compatible moisture needs, but polka dot plant grows taller and can shade fittonia if not positioned carefully.
Jewel orchids like the humidity moss provides, but need their crown kept clear of moss to avoid rot.

hard.label: Advanced
hard.description: Plants with narrow humidity/light/pH tolerances, slow growth, or conflicting needs with common terrarium moss. For experienced builders willing to monitor closely.

pairs.reason: Sundews need consistently moist, nutrient-poor, acidic substrate (typically sphagnum peat) — regular sheet moss and garden soil introduce nutrients that can kill carnivorous plant roots.
Sphagnum moss is the correct low-nutrient, acidic substrate carnivorous plants evolved with — but strict care is needed to keep it consistently damp, not soggy.
Same low-nutrient substrate requirement as sundews, but flytraps additionally need a genuine winter dormancy period most closed terrariums can't provide.
Java moss is adapted to aquatic/semi-aquatic conditions and holds more water than jewel orchid roots tolerate against the crown.
Mini begonias are highly prone to powdery mildew in the still, constantly humid microclimate moss creates without active airflow.
Correct acidic, low-nutrient substrate match, but bladderwort needs standing water at the base, which most terrarium builds don't account for by default.
Butterworts need excellent drainage and lower ambient humidity at the leaf surface than cushion moss's microclimate provides, risking leaf rot.
Alocasia is highly sensitive to overwatering and moss-driven excess moisture is a common cause of stem rot in enclosed setups.
Living sphagnum actively maintains the acidity and moisture band sundews need, but requires bright, indirect light most closed terrariums under-deliver.
Both are fast-spreading and moisture-competitive; selaginella's aggressive growth typically outcompetes delicate fittonia cultivars for root space within weeks.
